title: Chouquettes
subtitle: Light choux puffs rolled in coarse sugar
excerpt: Small, hollow choux pastry balls, piped in little mounds and rolled in coarse demerara sugar before baking until puffed and golden. Sold by the bag in every French boulangerie and almost unknown elsewhere. Best eaten within an hour of baking.
sourceNotes: Chouquettes are a French boulangerie staple documented in Auguste Escoffier, Le Guide Culinaire (1903), under the general category of pâte à choux preparations. The specific form of small choux puffs rolled in pearl sugar before baking is attested as a French confectionery tradition throughout the nineteenth and twentieth centuries. The recipe is fully public domain.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Choux pastry dough can be made up to two hours ahead and kept at room temperature. Pipe and bake just before serving; chouquettes soften quickly.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; conventional 220°C. Do not open the oven door during the first 15 minutes.

glossaryTerms.definition: A cooked paste of butter, water, flour, and eggs that puffs dramatically in the oven as steam from the high water content creates a hollow interior. Used for éclairs, profiteroles, and chouquettes.

content.text: Chouquettes are the simplest expression of

: no filling, no topping beyond a scatter of coarse sugar, no assembly. The whole point is the contrast between the crisp, sugar-encrusted shell and the hollow, eggy interior. They are eaten by the handful, warm, immediately after baking. They do not keep.

The choux method is straightforward once you understand what you're doing: cook butter and water together, add flour all at once and beat hard to form a smooth dough, then beat in eggs one at a time until the paste falls from the spoon in a soft V-shape. Too few eggs and the puffs will not rise; too many and the paste collapses flat. The V-shape test is the reliable indicator.

attrs.prepNote: for scattering over the puffs before baking

content.text: Heat the oven to 200°C fan (220°C conventional) and line two large baking trays with baking paper. Combine {{water}}, {{unsalted-butter}}, {{sea-salt-fine}}, and {{caster-sugar}} in a medium saucepan over medium heat. Bring to a full boil. Remove from the heat and add {{plain-flour}} all at once. Beat vigorously with a wooden spoon until the mixture forms a smooth ball that pulls away from the sides of the pan. Return to medium heat and stir for 1–2 minutes to dry the paste slightly. Transfer to a bowl and cool for 5 minutes. Add {{eggs}} a little at a time, beating well between each addition, until the paste is smooth, glossy, and drops from the spoon in a thick, reluctant ribbon — it should form a soft V when the spoon is lifted. You may not need all the egg.

Transfer the paste to a piping bag fitted with a 1 cm round nozzle or a large star nozzle. Pipe mounds of about 3 cm diameter onto the prepared trays, spacing them 4 cm apart. Scatter {{demerara-sugar}} generously over each mound immediately. Bake for 22–25 minutes until the puffs are deeply golden and feel hollow and light when lifted; the base should be dry and firm. Do not open the oven door for the first 15 minutes. Transfer to a cooling rack and eat within an hour.
If the puffs collapse after coming out of the oven, they were underbaked. Pierce the base of each puff with a skewer and return to the oven at 160°C fan for 5 minutes to dry the interior.
Pearl sugar (nib sugar) is the traditional topping and gives a more dramatic crunch than demerara. It can be ordered from baking suppliers.
The same choux paste can be piped into éclairs or into a ring for a simple paris-brest. Only the shape and garnish change.
